facts.question: What type of even numbers are hardest for Goldbach representations?
facts.correct: Numbers of the form n = 2p where p is a large prime, giving C(n) = 1
distractors: Highly composite numbers with many small prime factors
Powers of 2 because they have no odd prime factors
Numbers divisible by 6 because they share factors with many primes

facts.question: Among primes p giving the hardest 2p for Goldbach, what is the dominant residue class mod 30?
facts.correct: p equivalent to 19 mod 30, overrepresented 11:1 in the hardest numbers
distractors: p equivalent to 29 mod 30, the largest residue class
p equivalent to 1 mod 30, the quadratic residue class
All residue classes contribute equally to hard numbers

facts.question: Why does p mod 30 affect Goldbach difficulty when all mod-30 classes have the same number of valid CRT pairs?
facts.correct: It is a second-order distributional effect: p equivalent to 1 and 19 mod 30 have lower mean AND lower variance, concentrating them in the danger zone
distractors: Some mod-30 classes have more valid CRT pair channels than others
The Chebyshev prime race bias makes some classes have fewer primes
It is a numerical artifact that disappears at larger scales

facts.question: What is the per-factor variance compression of Goldbach residuals when controlled for n-size?
facts.correct: Approximately 21% reduction per additional odd prime factor, close to the CLT prediction from residue class counting
distractors: 66% reduction per factor from multiplicative sieve correlations
10% reduction per factor as predicted by basic sieve theory
No systematic reduction; variance is independent of factorization

facts.question: What is the sub-Poisson factor of Goldbach representation variance?
facts.correct: Var/Poisson ratio is 0.30 to 0.60, meaning representations are more regular than independent events due to shared prime supply
distractors: Var/Poisson ratio is exactly 1.0, consistent with independent Poisson process
Var/Poisson ratio is 2.0 to 3.0, indicating super-Poisson clustering
The variance follows a power law unrelated to Poisson statistics

facts.question: What causes the anti-bunching (negative lag-1 autocorrelation) in Goldbach residuals?
facts.correct: Shared prime supply constraint: the same primes serve as partners for multiple even numbers, so high r(n) depletes neighbors
distractors: Repulsive interaction between consecutive even numbers in the sieve
Numerical artifact of the Hardy-Littlewood normalization
The twin prime constant creating alternating high-low patterns

facts.question: What does the Eratosthenes sieve monotonicity property say about Goldbach coverage?
facts.correct: At every sieve step, removing multiples of the next prime never creates a new Goldbach failure; coverage is preserved from the trivial P=1 case through to the primes
distractors: The sieve creates temporary Goldbach failures that are repaired at later steps
Coverage decreases monotonically as the sieve removes more numbers
The sieve is only monotone for targets below the square of the current prime

facts.question: How do random sets compare to the deterministic sieve for Goldbach coverage?
facts.correct: Random sets of the same density have 20-36 Goldbach failures per trial; only the deterministic sieve has zero failures due to CRT structure
distractors: Random sets perform identically to the deterministic sieve
Random sets have fewer failures because they avoid systematic gaps
The deterministic sieve has more failures due to structured gaps

facts.question: What is the 2-adic correction to the Hardy-Littlewood prediction for Goldbach?
facts.correct: For n = 2^a times p, the normalized residual decreases with a: norm(a) approximately equals 1.605 + 0.196 times 0.304^a, saturating by a greater than or equal to 3
distractors: Powers of 2 have no effect on the Hardy-Littlewood prediction
Higher powers of 2 increase the normalized residual due to more even factors
The correction is linear in a, not exponential

facts.question: Does the Goldbach residual contain usable Liouville parity information?
facts.correct: The combined correlation is r = -0.014, statistically real but WEAKENS as n^{-0.60}, meaning the parity barrier becomes more absolute at larger scales
distractors: The parity signal is strong and grows with n, providing a path around the sieve barrier
There is zero parity signal in Goldbach residuals at any scale
The parity signal is constant regardless of n

facts.question: How does min/mean of r(2p)/E(2p) behave for the hardest Goldbach class?
facts.correct: It approaches 1 monotonically: 0.934 at p>1000, 0.962 at p>50000, 0.972 at p>400000, meaning the distribution concentrates
distractors: It decreases toward 0, suggesting eventual Goldbach failures
It oscillates without trend around 0.75
It stays constant at approximately 0.85 regardless of scale

facts.question: What is the mechanism behind the variance compression in Goldbach representations?
facts.correct: Residue class CLT: each odd prime factor of n creates (p-1) valid residue classes for Goldbach pairs, and pairs in different classes contribute approximately independently
distractors: Four-point primality correlations create strong inter-pair dependencies
The Riemann zeta function zeros directly modulate the variance
Multiplicative chaos from the singular series creates log-normal tails
